title: ci: audit stale.yml behavior on VS14's tiny issue set
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/stale.yml` runs daily (`0 0 * * *`). Latest run:
success. Unlike the other scheduled workflows, this one is doing
something — it uses `actions/stale@v9` to mark untriaged issues/PRs
stale after 90 days of inactivity.

```yaml
days-before-stale: 90
remove-stale-when-updated: true
only-issue-labels: 'S: Untriaged'
stale-issue-label: 'Stale'
days-before-issue-close: -1      ← never auto-close
```

## Why this needs assessment (not urgent but worth a look)
1. **It acts on our issues / PRs with real consequences**. Unlike
   the others, a misconfiguration here would spam stale labels on
   random tickets.
2. **VS14 has ~0 issues open right now**, so its impact is currently
   nil — but the moment we have any bug reports, they're subject to
   this automation.
3. **Upstream's label vocabulary** (`S: Untriaged`, `Stale`) was
   imported from Delta-V/SS14. If we haven't created those labels,
   the workflow quietly no-ops. Could be fine, or could silently
   mask that we should be triaging things.
4. **`days-before-issue-close: -1` = never close** is a safe
   default, but we should confirm we want that behavior (vs the
   more common "warn at 60, close at 90" pattern).

## Task
1. Check which labels currently exist in the repo
   (`gh label list --repo azigler/vacation-station-14`).
2. Create `S: Untriaged` and `Stale` if missing; otherwise the
   workflow is just a no-op every night.
3. Decide: keep "warn only, never close" (current), or adopt
   "close after N days" semantics.
4. Document the chosen policy in `CONTRIBUTING.md` or similar so
   people understand what triggers the Stale label.

## Options
### Option A: keep as-is + create labels
Current behavior is safe and matches SS14 convention. Just make
sure the labels exist so it's not a silent no-op.

### Option B: tune the thresholds
30 / 60 / 90 days depending on what fits the project cadence.

### Option C: disable
If we don't want any automation acting on our issue tracker.

## Recommendation
**A**. Create the labels (5-minute task), leave the behavior
alone, document it.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `S: Untriaged` and `Stale` labels exist in the repo
- [ ] Decision recorded on thresholds / close semantics
- [ ] CONTRIBUTING.md has a Triage Policy note
- [ ] Next scheduled run completes without action-required warnings
notes: SCOPE CHANGE post-Flavor-A: stale.yml is Delta-V-invented, NOT in SS14 upstream. Post-reset it'll be removed. Re-scope: decide whether to RE-INTRODUCE stale issue management via a VS14-authored workflow. Current scope (label audit) is moot because the labels won't be auto-referenced without the workflow.

title: impl: adapt SS14 grafana dashboards for VS14
description:
## Context
SS14 community provides two Grafana dashboards as JSON exports:
- Game Servers (uid K0LTdKmMk): fleet overview
- Perf Metrics (uid qyFqpFiGk): per-server deep dive with Logs panel

Source: docs.spacestation14.com/en/community/infrastructure-reference/grafana-dashboards.html

## Dependencies
- vs-2p3 (prometheus/loki/grafana stack) must be running

## Task
Adapt the upstream SS14 dashboards for Vacation Station. Store JSON in repo.
Provision automatically on Grafana startup via provisioning.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Download and adapt
- [ ] Fetch raw dashboard JSON from docs page
- [ ] Store at ops/grafana/dashboards/game-servers.json
- [ ] Store at ops/grafana/dashboards/perf-metrics.json

### Adaptations
- [ ] Replace datasource UIDs with our Grafana's
- [ ] Verify `job="gameservers"` matches our Prometheus scrape config
- [ ] Verify `server` label values match (our instance name)
- [ ] Rename where appropriate (e.g. "Wizden Servers" -> "Vacation Station")

### Provisioning
- [ ] ops/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml
- [ ] ops/grafana/provisioning/dashboards/dashboards.yml with file provider
  pointing at /var/lib/grafana/dashboards/
- [ ] docker-compose mounts dashboards + provisioning into Grafana container
- [ ] On fresh Grafana startup, both dashboards auto-appear

### Verification
- [ ] Game Servers dashboard shows our instance
- [ ] Perf Metrics dashboard's $Server variable populated with our instance name
- [ ] Logs panel on Perf Metrics shows live Loki logs
- [ ] Connections panel works (requires Postgres datasource configured)

### Documentation
- [ ] docs/OPERATIONS.md dashboard section
- [ ] Customization notes (where to edit for fork-specific metrics)
- [ ] How to add new panels or dashboards

human: advertise vacation-station on ss14 public hub
description:
## Context
Vacation Station 14 runtime config disables hub advertisement by default.
Players can still connect via direct URL (`ss14://51.81.33.136:1212`), but
the server won't appear in any launcher's public browser. Flip only when
ready for cold inbound traffic.

## Pre-flip checklist
- [ ] Run is stable: server has survived multiple full rounds without
      watchdog restart / crash-loop.
- [ ] Admin toolkit: at least one admin account seeded in postgres; admin
      commands (kick/ban/announce) tested from the client.
- [ ] **Rules & MOTD written** (see vs-rules bead) — hub launch with blank
      or upstream-default rules is a bad first impression.
- [ ] Auth decision made: flip to `mode = 1` (Required) before advertising
      unless you're consciously running a guest-friendly server. Guests
      via hub = grief farm in practice.
- [ ] vs-2y8 done (watchdog behind Caddy+HTTPS) — hub moderators and
      curious players will probe `:5000` and see plain HTTP.
- [ ] Discord crash alerts: `Notification.DiscordWebhook` populated in
      `/opt/ss14-watchdog/appsettings.yml` so we know when the server
      falls over.
- [ ] Capacity: enough resource headroom for first-wave hub traffic
      (recent metrics: tick rate, entity count, RSS under load). Cold
      starts from the hub can be 30+ players within minutes.

## Task — when ready
### Decide the hub list
Default SS14 hub: `hub.spacestation14.io` (Wizards Den). Adding additional
hubs is a comma-separated value in `hub.hub_urls`. Candidate hubs:

1. **Wizards Den** — `https://hub.spacestation14.io/` (primary, always include)
2. **Delta-V** — if Delta-V runs a distinct community hub, include it. VERIFY
   before listing. As of fork time we didn't confirm. Delta-V's server list
   lives somewhere public — check deltavstation.com or their Discord for
   the hub URL. If Delta-V uses Wizden hub only, skip.
3. **Starlight / other downstream communities** — some fork communities
   cross-list. Research before committing.
4. **Our own hub** — medium-term; we may eventually stand up a VS14-flavored
   hub that lists us plus sibling forks. Separate bead.

Multi-hub is free (each POST is independent) and discoverability-positive.
Being listed only on one hub means your server is invisible to clients
using other launchers configured against different hubs.

### Execute
1. Edit `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml`, find
   `[hub]` section.
2. Flip `advertise = true` (check the exact cvar name against the current
   template — might be `hub.advertise` or just `advertise` within the
   section).
3. Set `hub_urls` to the comma-separated list of hubs chosen above.
4. Confirm `server_url` is correct (the URL clients connect to after
   clicking through the browser). For now: `ss14://51.81.33.136:1212`.
   Once you have a domain, flip to `ss14://vacation.yourdomain.com:1212`.
5. Optionally set `tags` — e.g. `18+ rp-lite medium-pop hard-fork` — for
   launcher-side filtering.
6. Trigger server restart:
   ```
   curl -sS -u "vacation-station:$API_TOKEN" -X POST \
     https://<watchdog-url>/instances/vacation-station/restart
   ```
7. Verify:
   - Public SS14 launcher → server browser → "Vacation Station" appears
     within ~60s on each hub you listed.
   - Direct connect still works.
   - No hub-related errors in `journalctl -u ss14-watchdog --since '2 min ago'`.
   - Each hub's server list shows you at the expected tier (new, low-pop
     = sorted near bottom; that's normal).

### First-hour watch
Hub-new-server discovery wave can be 20-50 concurrent connections in the
first hour. Watch Grafana's Perf Metrics dashboard for tick rate drops,
entity count spikes, memory pressure. Be ready to flip Advertise back off
and POST /restart if the server's unstable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `hub.advertise = true` on the live host
- [ ] `hub.hub_urls` populated with every hub we want to list on
- [ ] Server visible in the SS14 launcher's public browser on each hub
      within ~5 minutes
- [ ] `server.rules` / `server.motd` populated and sensible
- [ ] At least one active admin account defined in postgres
- [ ] First-hour watch complete; no restart storm, no OOM, tick rate stable
- [ ] Record flip timestamp + launch-day traffic notes in this bead

## Delisting risk — read before flipping
The Wizden hub has a moderation team that can DELIST your server for:
- Inadequate rules / MOTD
- Obvious grief / no-RP angle
- Harassment or discriminatory content in server identity
- Impersonating another server
- Security issues that affect connecting clients (crashes, RCE)

Usually you'll get a warning first. Repeat offenses = removed without
notice. Build good ops habits now so you don't get caught out later.

## Notes
- Advertising is one-way-ish: you can flip it back off, but bookmarks
  persist. Changing the public IP / server_url is the only way to "hide"
  once out.
- Being on multiple hubs doesn't increase your grief risk — the SAME
  players see you via different launchers. It just reaches more people.

title: ops: assess + selectively fork ss14 ancillary services (cookbook, map-server, etc.)
description:
## Context
Ancillary SS14 ecosystem services assessed. This bead is the
**decision record**. Per-service work is tracked in child beads.

## Guiding principle (per user direction)

**External upstream lives as git submodules under `external/` in this
repo. Our config lives in `ops/<service>/` alongside. We only edit
code in this monorepo when customization is actually needed; until
then the submodule pins a specific upstream SHA and upstream LICENSE
files stay intact under that directory.**

This pattern:
- Extends the existing RobustToolbox-submodule approach to all third-
  party services
- Pins upstream SHAs (reproducibility + controlled updates)
- Co-locates code (submodule) + config (ops/) in one repo — no
  separate host-level clones to maintain
- Attribution / LICENSE compliance: upstream LICENSE files remain
  inside the submodule directory, automatically satisfying AGPL/MIT
  attribution rules
- Graduation to "monorepo fork" becomes explicit: `git rm` the
  submodule, copy the code into `services/<name>/`, start editing
  freely

## Decision matrix (final)

| Repo | Verdict | Child bead | Path in repo |
|---|---|---|---|
| [arimah/ss14-cookbook](https://github.com/arimah/ss14-cookbook) | submodule + config | vs-1vy | `external/cookbook/` + `ops/cookbook/` |
| [TrixxedHeart/ss14-paper-sim](https://github.com/TrixxedHeart/ss14-paper-sim) | SKIP | — | — |
| [yagwog/RMC14-document-simu](https://github.com/yagwog/RMC14-document-simu) | submodule + config | vs-v69 | `external/document-sim/` + `ops/document-sim/` |
| [space-wizards/SS14.MapServer](https://github.com/space-wizards/SS14.MapServer) | submodule + config | vs-236 | `external/map-server/` + `ops/map-server/` |
| [space-wizards/SS14.MapViewer](https://github.com/space-wizards/SS14.MapViewer) | rewrite in website | (vs-2dr) | `web/src/app/maps/` |
| [space-wizards/wizard-cogs](https://github.com/space-wizards/wizard-cogs) | inspect only | (vs-2l2) | — (port logic, don't vendor) |
| [space-wizards/SS14.Admin](https://github.com/space-wizards/SS14.Admin) | submodule + config | vs-35d | `external/admin/` + `ops/admin/` |
| [space-wizards/Robust.Cdn](https://github.com/space-wizards/Robust.Cdn) | defer | vs-3mv | (future: `external/cdn/` + `ops/cdn/`) |

## Repo layout (final)
```
vacation-station-14/
├── Content.{Server,Client,Shared}/     game code — owned, AGPL/MIT mix
├── RobustToolbox/                      submodule (engine, existing)
├── external/                           NEW — upstream submodules we deploy but don't edit
│   ├── cookbook/                       → arimah/ss14-cookbook
│   ├── map-server/                     → space-wizards/SS14.MapServer
│   ├── admin/                          → space-wizards/SS14.Admin
│   └── document-sim/                   → yagwog/RMC14-document-simu
├── web/                                Next.js site (vs-2dr), embeds MapViewer
├── services/                           our own code (edited)
│   └── discord-bot/                    (vs-2l2)
├── ops/                                configs + deploy artifacts per service
│   ├── observability/                  existing
│   ├── watchdog/                       existing
│   ├── postgres/                       existing
│   ├── cookbook/                       sources.yml, privacy.html, Caddy block, build script
│   ├── map-server/                     docker-compose, appsettings, GH App private-key location
│   ├── admin/                          docker-compose, appsettings, OAuth env file
│   └── document-sim/                   deploy script, Caddy block
└── docs/
    └── upstream-sync.md                tracks every submodule URL + pinned SHA
```

## Submodule lifecycle
### Adding
```bash
git submodule add https://github.com/<upstream>/<repo> external/<name>
git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/<name>.branch <main|master>
git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/<name>.ignore dirty
git add .gitmodules
git commit -m ":heavy_plus_sign: add external/<name> submodule pinning <sha>"
```

`ignore = dirty` matches RobustToolbox's config — prevents the
submodule's working-tree dirty-ness from leaking into our status.

### Updating (pin bump)
```bash
cd external/<name>
git fetch origin
git checkout <target-sha>
cd -
git add external/<name>
git commit -m ":arrow_up: bump external/<name> to <sha>"
```

### Promoting to in-tree fork (when we actually need to edit code)
```bash
# 1. Move code out of submodule tracking
git submodule deinit external/<name>
git rm external/<name>
rm -rf .git/modules/external/<name>

# 2. Copy the code into monorepo
git clone https://github.com/<upstream>/<repo> /tmp/<name>-work
cp -r /tmp/<name>-work/ services/<name>/  # or wherever it belongs
rm -rf services/<name>/.git

# 3. Commit as our own
git add services/<name>/
git commit -m ":arrow_up_small: promote external/<name> into services/<name> for <reason>"

# 4. Update docs/upstream-sync.md to reflect the new relationship
```

This is a deliberate, documented process. Don't promote casually —
every promotion adds a repo we have to keep in sync.

## `docs/upstream-sync.md` table shape
| Service | Upstream | Our mode | Path | Pinned SHA | Last sync |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Game | DeltaV-Station/Delta-v | hard-fork | repo root | n/a (merge) | |
| Engine | space-wizards/RobustToolbox | submodule | `RobustToolbox/` | (from gitlink) | |
| Cookbook | arimah/ss14-cookbook | submodule | `external/cookbook/` | | |
| MapServer | space-wizards/SS14.MapServer | submodule | `external/map-server/` | | |
| Admin | space-wizards/SS14.Admin | submodule | `external/admin/` | | |
| Doc-sim | yagwog/RMC14-document-simu | submodule | `external/document-sim/` | | |
| CDN | space-wizards/Robust.Cdn | deferred | n/a | n/a | n/a |

## `/upstream-sync` skill updates
Extend the skill to cover:
1. Periodic submodule updates (pin bumps)
2. Submodule conflict resolution during Delta-V merges
3. Promotion flow (external → services/)
4. License-compliance reminder on each bump

## Global acceptance criteria
- [x] Per-service verdict recorded with upstream URL
- [ ] `external/` directory created with its submodules
- [ ] `docs/upstream-sync.md` committed with all rows
- [ ] `/upstream-sync` skill updated for submodule workflows
- [ ] vs-3tq license audit passes on initial import

## License audit
Tracked in **vs-3tq** — each submodule must have its LICENSE file
retained at the path `external/<name>/LICENSE(.txt)` to satisfy MIT
attribution + AGPL notice retention. This is automatic with the
submodule approach since we're not modifying the upstream tree.
close_reason: Decision record complete. Per-service verdict matrix documented in description; implementation tracked via vs-1vy (cookbook), vs-236 (mapserver), vs-35d (admin), vs-v69 (doc-sim), vs-3mv (cdn-deferred), all parented under vs-2f8 (ops hardening).

title: ops: add services-flake dev stack (postgres + observability)
description:
## Context
Production bring-up of postgres + prometheus + loki + grafana is handled by the ops scripts + docker-compose we shipped (vs-3ty/vs-h3u/vs-2p3). That path is correct for the Ubuntu host. For local dev we want a zero-sudo, ephemeral equivalent so contributors can validate config changes without touching docker or a real host.

services-flake (https://community.flake.parts/services-flake) is a flake-parts module set that wraps process-compose to run a stack of services as user processes with data under a project-local dir.

Goal: `nix run .#dev-services` boots postgres + prometheus + loki + grafana on localhost against the SAME configs we committed in `ops/`, with data in `.data/` (gitignored).

## Dependencies
- vs-2ti (ops validation tools in dev shell) — not strictly blocking, but land that first so `promtool` etc. are available to validate configs the dev stack loads.

## Task
Extend `flake.nix` to add a services-flake output that composes:
- `services.postgres.pg1` — port 5432, database `vacation_station`, user `vs14`, password `dev-only-insecure` (literal — this is dev-only, document that)
- `services.prometheus.prom1` — config from `ops/observability/prometheus.yml` (or a dev-overlay that points the scrape target at the dev SS14 server port on localhost)
- `services.loki.loki1` — config from `ops/observability/loki-config.yml` (or dev-overlay as needed)
- `services.grafana.graf1` — admin password `admin` (dev only), provisioning dir pointing at `ops/observability/grafana/provisioning`, dashboards dir at `ops/observability/grafana/dashboards`

## Scope
1. **`flake.nix` inputs**: add `services-flake` (from `github:juspay/services-flake`) and `process-compose-flake` (`github:platonic-systems/process-compose-flake`).
2. **`flake.nix` outputs**: switch from `flake-utils.lib.eachDefaultSystem` to `flake-parts.lib.mkFlake` (services-flake requires flake-parts). Preserve the existing `devShells.default = import ./shell.nix { inherit pkgs; }` output — don't break the dev shell that's already working.
3. **New flake output**: `packages.<system>.dev-services` (or `apps.<system>.dev-services`) — the process-compose entrypoint.
4. **Service configs**:
   - Postgres: `initialDatabases = [{ name = "vacation_station"; }]` to match prod schema
   - Prometheus: load our committed `ops/observability/prometheus.yml`. If the `scrape_configs` target `host.docker.internal:44880` needs a dev override (docker-specific hostname won't resolve in dev), put a small nix-side overlay that rewrites the target to `localhost:44880`. Document this.
   - Loki: load `ops/observability/loki-config.yml`. If the `path_prefix` needs to be rewritten to a dev-local dir, do it in the nix expression.
   - Grafana: provisioning + dashboards from the committed paths. Datasource URLs point at the dev-stack services.
5. **Data persistence**: all services write state to `.data/{postgres,prometheus,loki,grafana}/` (project-relative). Update root `.gitignore` to ignore `.data/`.
6. **Docs**: add a "Dev Stack" subsection to `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` (create if absent) covering:
   - `nix run .#dev-services` to start
   - How to view logs (process-compose TUI)
   - How to reset (`rm -rf .data/`)
   - Credentials (dev-only literals; never reuse for prod)
   - Relationship to production (same configs, same schema, different storage)
   - SS14 connection: point game server's `config.toml` `[database]` at `localhost:5432` user `vs14` pw `dev-only-insecure`; `[metrics]` and `[loki]` already point at localhost.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `nix flake check` passes (or at minimum: `nix-instantiate --parse flake.nix` succeeds and `nix flake show` lists the new output)
- [ ] `nix run .#dev-services --help` (or equivalent) resolves — you don't need to actually boot the stack in the worktree, just prove the expression evaluates
- [ ] Existing `devShells.default` still evaluates (preserve `nix develop` behavior)
- [ ] `.data/` gitignored; no committed state files
- [ ] Dev-only credentials clearly commented as such in flake.nix and docs
- [ ] docs/DEVELOPMENT.md section documents bring-up, reset, credentials, schema-parity claim, and point back to vs-3ty/vs-2p3 for prod
- [ ] Single commit, `Bead: vs-1bb` trailer, gitmoji `:sparkles:`

## Out of scope
- Do NOT modify the production setup scripts or docker-compose
- Do NOT add SS14.Watchdog as a services-flake service (it's a single supervisor binary, not a service we'd want in dev alongside the stack — contributors run SS14 directly via `dotnet run` in dev)
- Do NOT attempt to actually boot the stack inside the worktree (process-compose wants a TTY and the services want ports; just prove the expression evaluates)

## Environment note
Prepend `eval "$(direnv export bash)" 2>/dev/null;` to nix commands so the flake toolchain is active. See CLAUDE.md "Nix env in worktree subagents".

## References to consult
- services-flake quickstart: https://community.flake.parts/services-flake
- Available services: https://community.flake.parts/services-flake/services
- flake-parts: https://flake.parts

## Commit
Example message:
```
:sparkles: ops: nix services-flake dev stack for postgres + observability

- flake.nix: add services-flake + process-compose-flake inputs, migrate to flake-parts
- packages.<system>.dev-services: postgres + prometheus + loki + grafana,
  data in ./.data/ (gitignored), binds to localhost, zero sudo required
- Services read the same configs committed in ops/ (with small dev overlays
  for host.docker.internal -> localhost and data paths)
- docs/DEVELOPMENT.md: Dev Stack section (bring-up, reset, credentials,
  schema parity with prod, how to wire a dev SS14 instance against it)
- devShells.default preserved unchanged
- .gitignore: .data/

Dev-only credentials embedded (documented as such). Production path is
unchanged (vs-3ty postgres + vs-2p3 docker-compose stack remain canonical).

Bead: vs-1bb
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

title: ops: fix cookbook build — recipe-gen.js TypeError on VS14 data
description:
## Context
First cookbook build after ops/cookbook/ scaffolding (vs-1vy) landed
with a runtime error in cookbook's recipe generator:

```
TypeError: o.values(...).filter is not a function
    at _ (/home/ubuntu/vacation-station-14/external/cookbook/bin/recipe-gen.js:1:7534)
```

The earlier pipeline stages succeed (schema generation parses 213
stacks, 281 construction graphs, 151 food sequence elements, 313
reactions, etc.). Failure is in the minified recipe generator, which
likely expects a data shape one of VS14's Resources/ YAMLs has
diverged from.

## Triage
1. Un-minify `recipe-gen.js` or pull from upstream source (`arimah/
   ss14-cookbook`) to find the call site.
2. Identify which data structure is being traversed (likely `recipes`
   or `microwaveMealRecipes` given the context).
3. Compare VS14's matching prototype YAMLs against what the code
   expects — probably a missing/renamed field from upstream SS14's
   recent schema evolution.
4. Either patch `ops/cookbook/rewrites_vacation.yml` to normalize
   the data, or open an upstream PR against `arimah/ss14-cookbook`.

## Status
- Build pipeline works end-to-end through schema gen; breaks at
  recipe gen.
- `/recipes/` on prod shows a "coming soon" stub.
- Not blocking anything else; purely gate on real recipe output.

## Parent
vs-1vy (cookbook deploy)
notes: Resolved 2026-04-13: root cause was Node.js 20 lacking Iterator Helpers (Map.values().filter is ES2024). Upgraded host to node 22.22.2 via nodesource; cookbook builds clean. Live at https://ss14.zig.computer/recipes/ (796K of generated content).

title: skill: /services — dev (nix services-flake) vs prod (systemd+docker) handbook
description:
## Context
We now have TWO parallel stack realities, and the lines are blurring in
conversations:

1. **Dev (nix services-flake)**: `nix run .#dev-services` boots
   postgres / prometheus / loki / grafana locally with state in
   `./.data/` (gitignored). Zero sudo. Insecure dev-only credentials
   literal in flake.nix. Data ephemeral by design — `rm -rf .data/` to
   reset.

2. **Prod (systemd + docker-compose)**: live on zig-computer right now.
   Postgres via apt + systemd. Watchdog / SS14.Server under
   ss14-watchdog.service. Observability via docker-compose in
   ops/observability/. Real credentials, persistent data in
   /var/lib/, /opt/, docker volumes. Reset = destructive surgery.

Each stack has conventions for bring-up, inspection, reset, and debugging
that are NOT interchangeable. Running dev commands against prod is
catastrophic; running prod commands against dev is usually pointless.
The existing `/nix` skill covers dev; prod is spread across docs/OPERATIONS.md
and tribal memory. Need a handbook.

## Task
Create `.claude/skills/services/SKILL.md`. Contents:

### 1. The two stacks — side-by-side table
Mirror what exists where: each service's dev location + prod location.
Example shape:

| Service | Dev | Prod |
|---|---|---|
| Postgres | services-flake `postgres.pg1`, `.data/postgres/` | apt `postgresql-17`, `/var/lib/postgresql/17/main/`, systemd |
| Prometheus | services-flake `prometheus.prom1` | docker-compose `prom/prometheus:v2.54.1` |
| Loki | services-flake `loki.loki1` | docker-compose `grafana/loki:3.2.1` |
| Grafana | services-flake `grafana.graf1` (:3200) | docker-compose `grafana/grafana:11.3.0` (:3200) |
| Watchdog | N/A (don't run in dev) | systemd `ss14-watchdog.service` |
| SS14 server | `dotnet run` directly | child of watchdog |

### 2. When to use which (decision tree)
| Goal | Stack | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Validate a `prometheus.yml` change | dev | ephemeral, fast reset, can't break prod |
| Test a DB migration | dev | same schema, guest-ignorable creds |
| Investigate a live bug | prod logs | dev reproduction rarely matches real traffic |
| Write a Grafana dashboard | dev first, copy to prod | dev is your scratchpad |
| Reset everything | see reset procedures |   |
| Test Discord webhook config | prod with a scratch channel | real webhook mechanics |

### 3. Bring-up procedures
**Dev** (full sequence):
```bash
eval "$(direnv export bash)"  # if not already
rm -rf .data                  # fresh state, optional
nix run .#dev-services
```

**Prod** (commands, not "walk-throughs" — the actual invocations you
repeat when something's been stopped):
```bash
sudo systemctl start postgresql ss14-watchdog ss14-backup.timer
cd /opt/vacation-station/ops/observability && docker compose up -d
```

### 4. Reset procedures
**Dev nuclear** (cheap, do whenever):
```bash
pkill -f process-compose
rm -rf .data
```

**Prod nuclear** (expensive, only when you've decided):
```bash
sudo systemctl stop ss14-watchdog
docker compose -f /opt/vacation-station/ops/observability/docker-compose.yml down
sudo -u postgres dropdb vacation_station
sudo -u postgres createdb -O vs14 vacation_station
sudo rm -rf /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/data/*
sudo rm -rf /var/backups/vacation-station/*   # ← consider archiving first!
docker volume rm vs14-observability_{prometheus,loki,grafana}-data
# then full bring-up per section 3
```

Also include **surgical resets** for individual services (just reset
Prometheus TSDB, just drop the DB, etc.).

### 5. Inspection (commands that are different between dev + prod)
- Process tree: dev = `process-compose attach`; prod = `systemctl status`,
  `docker compose ps`
- Logs: dev = process-compose TUI; prod = `journalctl --user -u X` /
  `docker compose logs X`
- Config reload: dev = Ctrl+C + re-run; prod = systemctl restart X /
  docker compose restart X (doesn't re-evaluate YAML unless recreated)
- Port checks: `ss -tln` works for both; the ports are (mostly) identical
  by design

### 6. Secrets handling — different rules
- Dev: literal in flake.nix, documented as dev-only
- Prod: generated on first install (postgres password, watchdog
  ApiToken, grafana admin). Captured in password manager once. Live
  only in `/etc/vacation-station/*.env` (gitignored) or inline in
  `/opt/.../config.toml`, `/opt/.../appsettings.yml` — NEVER in the
  repo

### 7. Gotchas we've hit
- Dev postgres data `/opt/vacation-station/.data/postgres` vs prod
  postgres data `/var/lib/postgresql/17/main` — utterly different;
  don't let scripts confuse them
- Dev Grafana at :3200, prod Grafana at :3200 — SAME PORT → they
  CAN'T run simultaneously on the same host. This is a feature.
- Config file paths: repo-committed templates end in `.example`;
  populated runtime versions are in `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/...`
  or `ops/observability/secrets/...`. Don't edit the `.example`
  expecting changes to take effect; it only propagates on fresh
  deploy.

### 8. Quick reference card
One-screen summary of the most-used commands, organized by verb (start,
stop, reset, log, exec into shell, check status).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `.claude/skills/services/SKILL.md` exists, follows the skill
      format (frontmatter with name/description/type)
- [ ] All 8 sections above are populated with real commands (copy-pasted
      from the actual host)
- [ ] Dev nuclear reset tested by running it; resulting fresh boot
      verified
- [ ] Prod nuclear reset procedures reviewed by a second person before
      ever executing (NEVER run this bead's prod reset as a test — it's
      destructive)
- [ ] Cross-link from `/nix` skill + `docs/OPERATIONS.md` pointing to
      this skill

## Out of scope
- Ops SLOs / incident runbooks — separate skill (once we have real
  production incidents to draw from)
- Cost monitoring (you'd want this once the VPS bill becomes a factor)

## Notes
- This skill should feel like an operator's cheat sheet, not a
  walkthrough. Assume the reader already understands the concepts;
  they need the commands fast.
notes: Post-Flavor-A: /services skill content is fork-agnostic. Dev stack (services-flake) already SS14-aware; prod stack ops are engine-level. No rewrite needed — the skill covers both dev and prod patterns that survive the reset unchanged.

title: ops: render SS14 guidebook to static HTML at /guidebook/
description:
## Context

SS14's Guidebook is an in-game knowledge base: Content.Client.Guidebook
renders markdown content from prototype definitions at
`Resources/Prototypes/Guidebook/*.yml`. Players press F1 or use the
in-game button to browse it. It covers game mechanics (how chemistry
works, airlock cycling, antag roles, etc.).

We already expose /recipes (cookbook), /maps (render index), /writer
(document-simu). /guidebook would complete the "reference material
accessible outside the game" trio.

## Scope

Following the same pattern as ops/cookbook/ and ops/map-render/:

1. `ops/guidebook/build.sh` — parse Resources/Prototypes/Guidebook/*.yml,
   extract embedded markdown content, render to HTML. Probably a
   dotnet tool or a python script walking the YAMLs.
2. Resolve SS14-specific markdown extensions:
   - `{texture:<sprite>}` — embed sprite previews
   - `{prototype:<id>}` — link to prototype pages (entities, reactions)
   - `{guidebook:<page>}` — inter-page links
3. Generate a static site with a navigation sidebar + per-page content
4. nginx location `/guidebook/` → alias `/var/www/vs14-guidebook/`
5. Systemd timer: daily rebuild (fast since no dotnet build required)
6. Update landing page (web/placeholder/index.html) with a
   Guidebook link

## Research questions

- Does SS14 expose a `Content.Guidebook.Renderer` or similar? If yes,
  use it (preserves all the in-game rendering behavior). If no, write
  a minimal markdown-to-HTML parser that handles the SS14 extension
  syntax.
- Do any existing forks serve their guidebook to the web? Check Wizden
  / Frontier / HardLight docs sites.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `/guidebook/` serves a navigable HTML version of VS14's
      in-game Guidebook
- [ ] Links between guidebook pages work
- [ ] Sprite previews render (even if lower-quality than the in-game version)
- [ ] Daily rebuild via systemd timer (pattern match cookbook)
- [ ] Landing page links to /guidebook/

## Parent
vs-z7v (community launch readiness)

## Blocks
None (can land anytime; low priority)

title: human: run vs-h3u watchdog setup on host
description:
## Context
vs-h3u shipped setup.watchdog.sh, systemd unit, appsettings.yml.example, instance-bootstrap.sh, and extended config.toml.example + docs/OPERATIONS.md. None of it has run on the host.

## Dependencies
- vs-y6o (postgres host setup) complete — watchdog unit has `After=postgresql.service`

## Task — run on the target host
1. `sudo ./setup.watchdog.sh` (optionally `WATCHDOG_REF=<tag-or-sha> sudo -E ./setup.watchdog.sh` to pin upstream).
   - Creates system user `ss14:ss14`
   - Publishes SS14.Watchdog to `/opt/ss14-watchdog/`
   - Installs `ss14-watchdog.service`, enables (not starts)
   - Generates ApiToken into a fresh `appsettings.yml` (only if absent)
2. Capture the generated ApiToken from `/opt/ss14-watchdog/appsettings.yml`. Store in password manager under "Vacation Station / watchdog / vacation-station ApiToken". Never commit.
3. `sudo ./ops/watchdog/instance-bootstrap.sh` — sets up `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/` layout and seeds `config.toml` from the template.
4. Edit `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml`: fill in postgres password from password manager; adjust hub/auth/status tags as desired.
5. Drop a Robust.Server build into `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/binaries/` (Local UpdateType needs this for first launch). Until we publish builds, a `dotnet publish` of our content + engine works.
6. Start the service: `sudo systemctl start ss14-watchdog`.
7. Verify:
   - `systemctl is-active ss14-watchdog` → active
   - `pstree -p $(pgrep -f SS14.Watchdog)` → shows `Robust.Server` child
   - `curl -sS http://localhost:5000/instances/vacation-station/ping` responds (exact path per watchdog version)
   - API auth: `curl -u vacation-station:$TOKEN -X POST http://localhost:5000/instances/vacation-station/restart` succeeds
   - Crash recovery: `sudo pkill -f Robust.Server`; confirm watchdog respawns it within TimeoutSeconds (default 60)
8. Firewall:
   - Confirm port 5000 NOT public (`ss -tln | grep :5000` shows 127.0.0.1 only; cloud firewall closed)
   - Confirm UDP 1212 + TCP 1212 open inbound per docs/NETWORKING.md (game server status + main port)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `systemctl is-active ss14-watchdog` → active
- [ ] Game server running as child of watchdog (pstree)
- [ ] ApiToken stored in password manager
- [ ] API ping + auth'd restart both succeed from localhost
- [ ] Crash recovery verified (kill Robust.Server, watchdog respawns)
- [ ] Port 5000 localhost-only; 1212 open per networking doc
- [ ] Record completion + timestamp in this bead before closing

## Notes
- Loki sink in appsettings.yml is intentionally commented out (waits on vs-2p3). Don't enable yet.
- UpdateType remains `Local` until we have a Manifest CDN. Don't change without planning an update pipeline.
- Report any divergence from setup.watchdog.sh so we can PR fixes back.

title: skill: /vibe-maintainer — maintainer playbook for the agent-era OSS norms
description:
## Context
Maintaining a public open-source project in 2026+ has different norms
than five years ago. Relevant article:
https://steve-yegge.medium.com/vibe-maintainer-a2273a841040

The specific tools and agent names in that article (Gas Town, Mayor,
etc.) are NOT what we're adopting. What we ARE adopting are the
emerging **norms** for AI-assisted OSS maintenance:

1. Expect that most incoming PRs will be AI-assisted. Plan for it
   rather than trying to ban it.
2. "Request changes, go fix your code" is a LAST resort, not a
   default. It starves contributors and they go fork you.
3. Instead, absorb the good parts of each PR, fix whatever else,
   and merge with **attribution preserved**.
4. Triage + review can be agent-assisted; the maintainer spends
   human judgment on the ambiguous subset (often the top 10%).
5. Rejection is serious. Every rejection raises the odds of a
   downstream fork. Reject when you mean it.
6. Solo maintainership + AI is a viable shape for a popular project
   at 2026 tooling levels. Don't pretend you have a review team
   you don't have.

VS14 is tiny today. If it grows, these norms become load-bearing.
Better to encode the pattern now while the board is small.

## Task
Create `.claude/skills/vibe-maintainer/SKILL.md` documenting:

### 1. Philosophy
- Default posture: absorb good from every PR, reject only as a
  principled last resort
- Preserve contributor attribution (git author, Co-Authored-By,
  changelog entry) when we fix-merge
- Optimize for community throughput, not per-PR perfection
- Solo maintainership with AI assistance is the assumption

### 2. Triage categories for incoming PRs
At first read, an agent bins each PR into one of:
- **Easy win**: targeted fix, doc update, dep bump, banned-author
  close, clearly-right contribution
- **Fix-merge candidate**: a small fix away from being an easy win
- **Needs deeper look**: ambiguous in substance or scope

### 3. Decision tree outcomes (10)
When reviewing a needs-deeper-look PR, the recommendation is one of:
1. Easy win (reclassify)
2. Merge (clean; ready)
3. Merge + follow-up (merge now, minor fixup in a subsequent commit)
4. Fix-merge (we pull locally, resolve issues, merge with
   attribution preserved)
5. Cherry-pick (take N of M items; discard rest politely)
6. Split-merge (decompose multi-concern PR; commit separately with
   attribution)
7. Redesign (problem worth solving; their design is wrong; we solve
   it our way; close PR with explanation)
8. Retire (obsolete / superseded; thank + close)
9. Reject (doesn't pay tech-debt weight; polite decline)
10. Request changes (LAST RESORT — documented as a concession to
    cases where contributor preference matters)

### 4. Hygiene rules for VS14 contributors
- Cross-fork pollution: keep `_VS/` separate from `_DV/`, `_NF/`, etc.
- Annotation: `// VS` / `// DV` / `// NF` for modifications to
  upstream-owned files
- No drafts (auto-closed)
- One concern per PR (prefer split-merge if violated)
- Rebase before submission
- Minimal changes — no gratuitous renames or reformats
- Include SS14 `:cl:` changelog block for player-facing changes
- Reference a bead ID for non-trivial changes

### 5. Fix-merge mechanics
When we fix a contributor's PR locally instead of sending it back:
- `git fetch origin pull/<N>/head:pr-<N>`
- `git checkout pr-<N>`
- Apply fixes — prefer small surgical changes
- `git rebase main`
- When committing fixes: `git commit --author="Original Author <email>"`
  OR add `Co-Authored-By:` trailer
- Merge via PR or direct push; close the original PR with a
  thank-you + summary of what we did
- Changelog entry credits original author (SS14 `:cl: OriginalName`)

### 6. Attribution discipline
- git author preserved on cherry-picked commits
- `Co-Authored-By:` trailer when we did conflict resolution
- `:cl:` block names the original author even when we fix-merged
- Commit subject includes `(from #<pr-number>)` reference for
  external-PR-origin commits

### 7. Security review for AI-generated contributions
Quick checklist the agent runs on suspicious PRs:
- Any new secrets / tokens / credentials in the diff?
- Any new network endpoints, especially outbound?
- Any new dependencies added? Pinned to specific versions?
- Any auth / identity / admin-path changes?
- Any base64-encoded blobs without clear purpose?

If any hit: escalate to human before merging.

### 8. When to escalate to human
- Substantial architectural changes
- Anything matching the security checklist
- Controversial design calls (PR comments disagree with us)
- Unknown contributor with sweeping changes
- Anything that touches `_DV/` or `_NF/` in a way that conflicts
  with upstream sync expectations

### 9. Cross-references
- Pairs with `/commit` (for our own commits)
- Pairs with `/orchestrator` (agent roles for review + fix-merge)
- Pairs with `/review` (deeper bead-level review)
- Paired bead: vs-2sr (CONTRIBUTING.md) — contributors see this
  skill reflected in our documented hygiene
- Paired bead: vs-f0l (scheduled PR triage workflow) — CI-side
  automation for the "easy win" half of the decision tree

## Acceptance
- [ ] `.claude/skills/vibe-maintainer/SKILL.md` exists
- [ ] All 9 sections above populated with concrete examples
- [ ] Attribution/fix-merge procedures have copy-pasteable commands
- [ ] Cross-links to CONTRIBUTING.md and related skills
- [ ] The skill reads as principles + procedures, not tool-specific
      (no Gas Town branding)

## Dependencies
- Blocks on vs-ddu.2 (post-Flavor-A base state before this skill's
  procedures are codified)

## Not in scope
- Full multi-agent orchestration frameworks (Mayor/polecats/etc)
  — our scale doesn't justify it; our existing /orchestrator is
  enough
- Trust-ranking systems / contributor rep scores
- Automated fix-merge without human in loop (too risky at our scale)
notes:
## Framing note (per user direction)

This skill is NOT about adopting any specific tool or orchestration
framework from external references. It's about acknowledging that the
maintainer / contributor / user dynamics have all shifted in the
agent era, and codifying how WE operate under the new normal.

The shifts:
- **Maintainer**: one human + AI assistance; no volunteer review team
- **Contributor**: AI-assisted humans (or AI agents); higher velocity;
  submission effort is low so expectation is higher
- **User**: expects fast response, low friction, and will fork if we
  drag; every popular project is a credible fork target now

The skill captures: a clear decision tree for what to do with
incoming PRs, attribution discipline when we fix-merge, hygiene rules
for contributors, and escalation criteria for human-only judgment
calls. Reference article (Yegge's "Vibe Maintainer") is useful
background, NOT a methodology to copy.

title: TEST: epic type smoke test
ops: cherry-pick + curate GitHub Actions from upstream forks (agent-supporting)
description:
## Context
After Flavor A Phase 1 lands, our `.github/workflows/` contains
pure upstream SS14's 24 workflows (we documented the drift in
vs-345 — Delta-V added stale.yml, SS14 has labeler-stable/staging +
publish-testing Delta-V didn't). Now we re-examine every workflow
under the vibe-maintainer lens and curate deliberately rather than
inheriting.

## Workflow audit matrix (post-Flavor-A)

Every workflow falls into one of these categories. DECISION required
per workflow.

### Quality gates (KEEP — essential regardless of community size)
| Workflow | Purpose | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| `yaml-linter.yml` | Prototype YAML validation | **KEEP** — invaluable for content authors (us included) |
| `check-crlf.yml` | Line-ending sanity | **KEEP** — cheap, catches Windows cross-editor issues |
| `rsi-diff.yml` | Visual sprite-diff in PR comments | **KEEP** — huge win for agent + human review of visual changes |
| `validate-rsis.yml` | RSI metadata integrity | **KEEP** |
| `validate-rgas.yml` | RGA schema validation | **KEEP** |
| `validate_mapfiles.yml` | Map file schema | **KEEP** |
| `build-test-debug.yml` | Compile + unit tests | **KEEP** — the ground-truth quality gate |
| `test-packaging.yml` | Ensure release packaging succeeds | **KEEP** — vs-ddu.4 learnings |
| `no-submodule-update.yml` | Block accidental submodule bumps | **KEEP** — our pre-commit hook peer |
| `build-map-renderer.yml` | Map preview generation | **KEEP** — supports /maps route in website |

### Publishing (KEEP — we distribute builds)
| Workflow | Purpose | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| `publish.yml` | Production release artifact | **KEEP** — needed if we stand up Robust.Cdn (vs-3mv) |
| `publish-testing.yml` | Testing / beta builds | **KEEP** — staging lane for cherry-picks |

### Community gates (EVALUATE carefully — solo-maintainer reality)
| Workflow | Purpose | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| `labeler-pr.yml` | Auto-apply type labels | **KEEP** — helpful for /vibe-maintainer sheriff |
| `labeler-size.yml` | S/M/L size labels | **KEEP** — essential for sheriff triage |
| `labeler-conflict.yml` | Merge-conflict detection | **KEEP** — high signal for fix-merge path |
| `labeler-needsreview.yml` | Mark needs-review state | **KEEP** |
| `labeler-untriaged.yml` | Default state label | **KEEP** |
| `labeler-stable.yml` | Publication channel tag | **DEFER** — relevant if we do channeled releases |
| `labeler-staging.yml` | Publication channel tag | **DEFER** |
| `labeler-review.yml` | Approved-label bot | **DISABLE** — requires LABELER_PAT secret; solo maintainer doesn't benefit |
| `close-master-pr.yml` | Auto-close wrong-branch PRs | **KEEP** — cheap cleanup |
| `update-credits.yml` | Contributor list auto-append | **KEEP** — guard to our repo name; light attribution win |

### Documentation (EVALUATE — do we run a docs site?)
| Workflow | Purpose | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| `build-docfx.yml` | Generate + publish DocFX docs | **DISABLE** — no docs site; vs-352 player docs go through website not docfx |

### Infra-coupled (SKIP — not our infrastructure)
| Workflow | Purpose | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| `benchmarks.yml` | Perf bench on centcomm | **DISABLE** — centcomm is Wizden; vs-zyk documented skip |

## Additional workflows to ADD (from sibling forks)

From Frontier's `.github/workflows/`:
- `changelog.yml` / `publish-changelog.yml` — pairs with SS14.Changelog
  webhook (vs-3v4); consider as alternative to the webhook for
  post-merge Changelog.yml updates without running a hosted service.
  Decide after vs-3v4 is deployed.

From Einstein-Engines:
- `prtitlecase.yml` — validates PR title case. Nice-to-have; advisory
  only, not blocking. **ADOPT IF LIGHTWEIGHT**.
- `discord-changelog.yml` — Discord announcement pipeline. Pairs with
  vs-2l2; consider once Discord bot phases are live.

From this work, NET changes post-Flavor-A:
- DISABLE (via `gh api .../disable`): labeler-review, build-docfx, benchmarks
- DEFER (no action): labeler-stable, labeler-staging (re-evaluate if
  we stand up channeled releases)
- ADOPT (cherry-pick / port): prtitlecase (low-risk), maybe changelog.yml
- SS14's all others: KEEP as-is

## Task
1. Execute the decisions in the matrix above, via the vs-345 epic's
   existing per-workflow child beads (vs-j78, vs-12b, vs-39z, vs-367,
   vs-zyk — some may fold in or spawn siblings).
2. For each DISABLE decision: `gh api -X PUT /repos/.../actions/workflows/<id>/disable`
3. For each ADOPT decision: copy workflow file in; test via workflow_dispatch
4. Update `docs/OPERATIONS.md` with a "CI Workflows" section listing
   every active workflow + its purpose + who it notifies

## Acceptance
- [ ] Matrix above is executed — every workflow has a decision applied
- [ ] Agent-supporting additions (prtitlecase if chosen) working
- [ ] docs/OPERATIONS.md "CI Workflows" section complete
- [ ] First post-curation PR runs only the intended workflows
- [ ] vs-345's child beads (vs-j78, vs-12b, vs-39z, vs-367, vs-zyk)
      all closed through this work

## Dependencies
- Blocks on vs-ddu.2 (clear commit resets workflows to SS14's)
- Blocks on vs-1ss (/vibe-maintainer skill — decision framework)

## Out of scope
- Custom benchmarking infrastructure (separate bead if we ever want it)
- Custom docs site (separate bead; vs-352 explores via wiki)
- Canary / staged rollouts (separate bead; tied to Robust.Cdn vs-3mv)

## Notes
- This bead replaces the scattered individual workflow-decision beads
  with a single audit-and-execute pass. The individual beads stay open
  for per-workflow tracking but close as this pass completes them.
notes:
## Framing note
The audit isn't "which workflows to keep from Delta-V vs SS14" — it's
"which workflows make sense for a solo + AI maintainer in 2026+."
Some inherited workflows assumed a multi-person review team with a
shared PAT-bot; those get disabled. Quality gates stay (they protect
everyone regardless of team shape). New workflows get added to emit
agent-readable context the maintainer's review agent needs.

The matrix in the main description categorizes each workflow
accordingly.

title: legal: public-facing TOS, privacy policy, DMCA contact, data retention
description:
## Context
The moment VS14 is public — hub-advertised, collecting player accounts,
retaining logs, running Discord — legal obligations attach. Different
jurisdictions apply based on hosting location (VPS in France per the
51.81.x.x IP block, so French + EU data protection law applies), user
location (GDPR-reach is worldwide for EU residents), and operator
location (you).

Blank/absent legal framing is fine for an invite-only test. For a
public hub launch it's minimally negligent and potentially actively
harmful (liability, DMCA takedowns with no process).

## Task — minimum viable legal framework

### 1. Terms of Service
`docs/community/legal/tos.md` + published at `vacation.<domain>/tos`.
Cover at minimum:
- Acceptance by connecting / using the service
- Age requirement (13+ COPPA, 16+ for EEA per GDPR)
- Acceptable use (cross-reference server rules)
- Account termination rights (bans — we do this; TOS grounds us)
- IP assignment (none — users keep their words; we don't claim their chat)
- Disclaimer of warranty (game is provided as-is)
- Limitation of liability (we're not responsible for damages)
- Change-of-terms procedure
- Governing law (France / where you operate)

### 2. Privacy Policy
`docs/community/legal/privacy.md` + `vacation.<domain>/privacy`.
Required by GDPR, CCPA, and basic decency. Cover:
- What we collect: username (Wizden OAuth'd or guest), IP address
  (logged transiently by watchdog), chat logs, admin actions, Discord
  account if they join, round replays (sensitive — see vs-2l1)
- Why we collect each: moderation, crash debugging, gameplay
- Retention period for each type: chat logs 30d, admin actions
  indefinite (required for ban appeals), replays variable (tie to
  vs-2l1 retention), Discord data indefinite while member
- Third parties: Wizards Den (identity/auth), Discord (their platform's
  TOS applies), VPS host (incidental, DPA in place)
- User rights: access, export, deletion (how to request)
- Data controller contact (email)

### 3. DMCA / Takedown process
`docs/community/legal/dmca.md` — required if we host user content
(replays, map submissions). Even if we think it's unlikely someone'd
DMCA us, having a process means:
- A named agent to receive notices (email, postal address)
- Procedure for takedown + counter-notice
- Safe-harbor compliance so we're not liable for user infringement

### 4. Data Retention Policy (internal-facing, but publishable)
`docs/community/legal/retention.md`:
- Which data, how long, why, deletion mechanism
- Ban-evasion / permanent-identifier policy
- Backup retention (pg_dumps held 28 days, then gone)
- Log retention (journal 30d, syslog 5w)
- Replay retention (14d raw / 180d metadata — per vs-2l1)
- Incident retention (security events held 1y)

### 5. Cookie Policy (if the website uses them)
If vs-2dr uses any analytics cookies, first-party auth cookies for
admin, etc., need a cookie banner + policy.

## Execution
1. Start with templates (Termly, iubenda, or public-domain templates
   from the EFF). Do NOT just copy from another server.
2. Have the templates reviewed BY A LAWYER if we expect meaningful
   player volume or any donations. Free/template legal is a starting
   point, not an endpoint.
3. Publish on website under `/legal/*` routes.
4. Link from website footer + Discord #rules + game MOTD.
5. Name a DPA (data protection officer) contact — likely yourself
   for now.

## Acceptance
- [ ] TOS published
- [ ] Privacy Policy published with real retention numbers
- [ ] DMCA process documented with named agent
- [ ] Data retention policy documented + matches actual infra
      retention (backup cron, journald caps, replay retention)
- [ ] Legal footer linked from website, Discord, game MOTD
- [ ] Optional: lawyer review commissioned if scope justifies

## Dependencies
- vs-2dr (website serves /legal pages)
- vs-2l1 (replay retention policy feeds data retention)
- All infra retention decisions (we should be honest about what we
  actually keep and for how long)

## Out of scope
- Full incorporation / business entity setup (if VS14 becomes a real
  non-profit or company later, separate bead)
- EULA for the game engine (that's RobustToolbox's responsibility)

## Notes
- Boring but necessary. Don't skip.
- GDPR fines are per-record for noncompliance. Don't collect what you
  don't need.
- Transparency = trust. Under-promise retention (say 30d, actually
  keep 14d) — hard to get in trouble for deleting TOO much.

title: ops: deploy vanilla ss14-cookbook configured for VS14
description:
## Context
Parent: **vs-19h** (decision matrix + submodule convention).

Use arimah/ss14-cookbook as-is (no fork) via submodule at
`external/cookbook/`. VS14-specific config lives in `ops/cookbook/`.
Build on deploy; serve static output via Caddy.

## Task
1. Add submodule:
   ```
   git submodule add https://github.com/arimah/ss14-cookbook external/cookbook
   git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/cookbook.branch main
   git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/cookbook.ignore dirty
   git add .gitmodules
   git commit -m ":heavy_plus_sign: add external/cookbook submodule"
   ```
2. Add `ops/cookbook/`:
   - `sources.yml` — points at VS14's Resources/ (via a sibling
     read-only clone of VS14 maintained on the host, since cookbook
     needs yaml+png files, not nix-built output). Path variable.
   - `privacy.html` — our privacy notice (derives from vs-1ux).
   - `rewrites_vacation.yml` — per-VS14 recipe rewrites (empty at
     first; populate when we spot recipes that don't render).
   - `build.sh` — wraps `npm ci && npm run build && npm run gen:recipes`,
     rsync's `external/cookbook/public/` to the served dir (e.g.
     `/var/www/cookbook.vacation.<domain>/`).
   - `Caddyfile.block` — snippet for `cookbook.vacation.<domain>`.
   - `vs14-cookbook-build.service` + `.timer` — daily rebuild
     triggered systemd unit.
3. Sibling clone: the cookbook needs a separate VS14 checkout to
   parse (it reads Resources/ files directly; shouldn't share with
   our dev tree). Script in `ops/cookbook/` that clones / updates a
   read-only mirror at `/var/lib/vs14-cookbook-source/`.
4. AGPLv3 compliance: the deployed cookbook's footer must include a
   "View Source" link pointing at `github.com/azigler/vacation-station-14`.
   Verify the template either has a footer-customization option or
   do a small post-build patch (belongs under Phase 2 if post-build
   patch required — record as promotion trigger).

   listing VS14 so we appear on ss14.recipes.
6. Update `docs/upstream-sync.md` with cookbook row.

- vs-1ux (privacy policy text)
