title: ops: watchdog path rename /admin → /watchdog + purge community-research from history
description:
## Combined op — single force-push to main

This bead captures the multi-step cleanup triggered by:

1. User feedback that nginx `/admin/` is the wrong path for the
   watchdog API now that SS14.Admin (vs-35d) is queued, and "path
   over subdomain" is the consistent VS14 pattern.
2. User decision that `docs/community-research.md` (agent-produced
   synthesis of inter-fork community patterns) is too candid to live
   in public git history; move to the private `azigler/research-ss14`
   repo + purge from VS14's history.
3. Three parallel worktree agents (cookbook / mapviewer / dashboard)
   completed while the above decisions landed; all three need
   merging in the same force-push.

## Procedure

### 1. nginx: rename `/admin/` → `/watchdog/`
- Edit `ops/nginx/ss14.zig.computer.conf`: `location /admin/` →
  `location /watchdog/`. Proxy target stays `http://127.0.0.1:5000/`.
- `sudo install -m 0644 ...` to deploy, then `sudo certbot --nginx -d
  ss14.zig.computer --reinstall` + `sudo systemctl reload nginx`.
- Smoke-test: `curl -I https://ss14.zig.computer/watchdog/` →
  expects 401 (watchdog ApiToken).

### 2. Doc updates for the path rename
- `docs/NETWORKING.md` — any `/admin/` → `/watchdog/` references;
  update the "What's behind nginx vs. what isn't" table + verify
  commands.
- `docs/OPERATIONS.md` — any operator commands curl'ing `/admin/` →
  `/watchdog/`.
- `ops/nginx/ss14.zig.computer.conf` header comment — `/admin/` line
  becomes `/watchdog/`.
- `web/placeholder/index.html` — not affected (no watchdog link in
  the placeholder).
- Bead notes: `vs-35d` Redirect URI example that mentioned `/admin2/`
  no longer needed; now `/admin/` is free for SS14.Admin itself.
- Clarify in `vs-35d` that subpath `/admin/` is now the recommended
  target for SS14.Admin (path parity with /recipes, /maps).

### 3. Remove community-research from VS14
- `git rm docs/community-research.md` (keep ~/ss14 / research-ss14
  copy).
- Scrub the `see docs/community-research.md` reference inside
  `vs-2l2` bead notes; replace with `see research-ss14 repo
  (private)`.

### 4. History rewrite
- `git filter-branch --index-filter "git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch
  docs/community-research.md" -- --all` (fast; index-only).
- Prune refs, re-gc, force-push.

### 5. Merge the three worktree agents
- `worktree-agent-a845e3c5` (vs-2du.1 dashboard) — ALREADY merged at
  HEAD~5 (`6a22638758`); trivially falls out of the filter-branch.
- `worktree-agent-a7630562` (vs-1vy cookbook) — already merged at
  HEAD~2; same.
- `worktree-agent-a3c5321d` (vs-236 mapviewer) — merge commit at
  HEAD; same.
- filter-branch preserves merge commits; no re-merge needed.

### 6. Force-push
- `git push --force-with-lease origin main`
- Also force-push tags (pre-flavor-a-clear + flavor-a-baseline-
  2026-04-12 — these are unaffected since they predate the research
  file, but we push tags in case filter-branch touches anything).

### 7. Close dependent beads
- `vs-1vy` (cookbook) — closed on merge
- `vs-236` (mapviewer) — closed on merge
- `vs-2du.1` (dashboard) — closed on merge
- This bead — closed when force-push succeeds + smoke-tests pass

### 8. Cleanup
- `git worktree remove --force .claude/worktrees/agent-<id>` for the
  three agents
- `git branch -D worktree-agent-<id>` local + push deletion
- .claude/worktrees/ dir cleaned

## Path-decision reference

For future beads to cross-link: VS14 uses **path-based routing** as
the consistent pattern for adding services to `ss14.zig.computer`.
One domain, one cert, paths grow as services land:

| Path | Service | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `/` | placeholder landing (vs-2dr) | live |
| `/recipes/` | ss14-cookbook (vs-1vy) | alias ready, build pending |
| `/maps/` | SS14.MapViewer (vs-236) | alias ready, build pending |
| `/instances/` | Robust.Watchdog download passthrough | live |
| `/client.zip` | convenience download alias | live |
| `/watchdog/` | Robust.Watchdog admin API | live after this bead |
| `/admin/` | SS14.Admin UI (vs-35d) | reserved, unblocks when Wizden OAuth registered |

Subdomains only if a service genuinely can't live under a path
(currently: none do).

## Acceptance

- [ ] `/admin/` route removed from nginx vhost (committed + pushed)
- [ ] `/watchdog/` route works on prod (smoke-test returns 401 at `/watchdog/`)
- [ ] Docs reflect new path
- [ ] `docs/community-research.md` gone from `git log`
- [ ] Force-push succeeded
- [ ] Three agent beads closed cleanly
- [ ] Worktrees + remote worktree branches cleaned up

title: ops: player-facing docs / wiki for VS14
description:
## Context
Players need documentation: how to play, job tutorials, crafting, antag
guides, station rules (vs server rules), admin contact procedures.
Upstream SS14 wiki (wiki.spacestation14.io) is the default for vanilla
mechanics; VS14's hard-fork divergence means some of that becomes
subtly-to-severely wrong for us over time.

## Task
1. **Platform decision**:
   - Option A: extend `vs-2dr` website with `/docs` route + MDX
     rendering of markdown files under `docs/player/`. Simple, editable
     via PR, versioned with the game code.
   - Option B: self-hosted wiki (Wiki.js, Outline, Bookstack) that
     community members can edit without a PR.
   - Option C: vanilla — link out to upstream SS14 wiki and add a
     small "VS14 differences" page.
   **Recommend B (Wiki.js or Outline)** for long-term — wiki-style
   editing scales with community contributors; docs-in-repo does not.
   Start with a skeleton and migrate pages in as authors write them.
2. **Initial structure**:
   - Getting Started (connecting, launcher, first round)
   - Jobs (one page per job — Cargo, Security, Engineering, Medical,
     Science, Service, Command)
   - Crafting / Cookbook (if vs-1vy cookbook ships, link to it; else
     page-per-workbench)
   - Antag guides (Traitor, Ninja, Nukie, etc. — carefully: too much
     detail undermines mystery)
   - Admin / moderation (how to contact, what admins do, appeal
     process — linked from ban reasons)
   - VS14 differences from upstream SS14 (living list of what we've
     changed, linked from any page whose content diverges)
   - Contributing (how to write wiki pages, style guide)
3. **Ops**:
   - Host on same VPS: `wiki.vacation.<domain>` with Caddy HTTPS
   - Postgres-backed (share the DB or separate instance — decide based
     on load expectations; start shared with its own schema)
   - Backup integrated into the existing pg_dump pipeline
4. **Content seeding**: populate the 5-10 most-important pages first.
   The rest grow organically as community contributes.

## Acceptance (Phase 1)
- [ ] Platform chosen + deployed
- [ ] Caddy + HTTPS configured
- [ ] Initial page skeleton created
- [ ] 5+ pages populated by launch
- [ ] Contributor onboarding doc written
- [ ] Linked from website nav + Discord #welcome

## Dependencies
- vs-2y8 (Caddy)
- vs-2dr (website — for navigation integration)
- vs-3e3 (content) — rules / positioning could live on wiki too, but
  SSOT stays in docs/community/ and wiki PULLS from there

## Out of scope
- Migrating the entire upstream SS14 wiki (impossible and pointless).
  Own what diverges; link out for what doesn't.
- Approval workflows for every edit — if the wiki gets abused we can
  add them then.

## Notes
- Wiki.js supports git sync with markdown files, which could let us
  keep docs-as-code AND wiki editing. Worth evaluating.
- Don't promise exhaustive docs at launch. Promise "the important
  stuff, growing over time."

ops: deploy vanilla SS14.Admin with Wizden OAuth + our postgres
description:
## Context
Parent: **vs-19h** (decision matrix + submodule convention).

Deploy space-wizards/SS14.Admin as-is via submodule at
`external/admin/`. VS14-specific config in `ops/admin/`. Stay on
Wizden OAuth per user direction.

## Task
1. Add submodule:
   ```
   git submodule add https://github.com/space-wizards/SS14.Admin external/admin
   git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/admin.branch master
   git config -f .gitmodules submodule.external/admin.ignore dirty
   ```
2. `ops/admin/`:
   - `docker-compose.yml` — `build: context: ../../external/admin`,
     bind `127.0.0.1:<port>`, EnvironmentFile for secrets.
   - `appsettings.Production.yaml.example` — with:
     ```yaml
     Authentication:
       Oidc:
         Authority: "https://central.spacestation14.io/web/"
         ClientId: "<from Wizden registration>"
         # ClientSecret loaded via env, NOT in file
     ConnectionStrings:
       DefaultConnection: "Host=host.docker.internal;Database=vacation_station;Username=vs14;Password=<env>"
     PathBase: "/admin"
     ```
   - `Caddyfile.block` — `admin.vacation.<domain>` or
     `vacation.<domain>/admin` → `127.0.0.1:<port>` with HTTPS.
3. Register OAuth client with Wizden's identity provider. Store
   ClientId + ClientSecret in `/etc/vacation-station/admin-oauth.env`
   (gitignored). systemd EnvironmentFile loads into the container.
4. Seed initial admin via SQL against `admin` table after first
   migration run (add your Wizden UUID).
5. `docs/OPERATIONS.md` section on admin-panel ops, OAuth client
   rotation, admin-seeding.
6. Update `docs/upstream-sync.md`.

## Acceptance
- [ ] `external/admin/` submodule
- [ ] docker-compose stack boots against live vs14 postgres
- [ ] Wizden OAuth sign-in works
- [ ] ≥1 admin account active
- [ ] Kick / ban / admin-log endpoints functional
- [ ] Caddy-fronted HTTPS
- [ ] OAuth secret in env file, gitignored

## Promotion triggers
- Custom admin panels for VS14-specific features
- UI rebranding beyond config
- Our own identity provider (big, separate)

## Dependencies
- vs-2y8 (Caddy) — admin MUST be HTTPS
- vs-3ty done (postgres)
- OAuth client registered with Wizden

## Security
- Admin panel = high-value target; HTTPS non-optional
- Consider Caddy basic_auth as extra layer on top of OIDC
- Rotate client secret on any suspicion of compromise
- postgres admin user must NOT be superuser; scope grants to what
  SS14.Admin's migrations need
notes:
## Progress 2026-04-13

OAuth registration DONE via Wizden self-service portal (contrary to
earlier assumption that it required manual ask). Creds stashed on
host at ``/etc/vacation-station/admin-oauth.env`` (mode 640,
root:ss14). Do NOT copy these creds into the repo or into systemd
unit text; reference only via EnvironmentFile / docker env_file.

**Rotation-after-deploy**: the secret transited a Claude chat
transcript and should be rotated via the Wizden portal once the
panel is confirmed working. Track as a follow-up.

## First-admin bootstrap (chosen path)

Deploy panel with NO seeded admin. User logs in → OIDC succeeds but
admin-check fails visibly. Operator extracts user's hub UUID from
SS14.Admin logs (``docker compose logs ss14-admin | grep
<username or sub claim>``), seeds via:

    UPDATE admin SET flags = <god-level-mask>
      WHERE user_id = '<uuid>';

Document this in docs/OPERATIONS.md.

## Redirect URI (confirmed)

``https://ss14.zig.computer/admin/signin-oidc`` (per SS14.Admin
README, not the earlier ``/Identity/Account/ExternalLogin`` note).

## DB

Same database as game server: ``vacation_station`` on the host
postgres, owner vs14. SS14.Admin's migrations extend the existing
schema; no separate DB needed.

title: ops: cross-platform nix dev shell (darwin + windows via wsl2)
description:
## Context
The nix flake (`flake.nix`) currently restricts to `x86_64-linux` + `aarch64-linux`. `shell.nix` pulls libdrm/mesa/xorg/gtk3 — all Linux-only — which causes `nix flake check` to fail on darwin. Windows lacks native nix entirely; any nix-based dev path must go through WSL2.

Goal: every platform gets a usable nix-first dev experience, aligned with the first-class nix stance.

## Task

### macOS (darwin): real flake support
1. Split `shell.nix` into platform-aware structure. Options:
   - (a) `shell.nix` takes a `pkgs` arg, branches on `pkgs.stdenv.isDarwin` / `isLinux`, conditionally includes graphics libs.
   - (b) Factor out `shell/common.nix`, `shell/linux.nix`, `shell/darwin.nix`; `shell.nix` selects based on system.
   Prefer (a) — less file sprawl. The branching is `lib.optionals pkgs.stdenv.isDarwin [...] ++ lib.optionals pkgs.stdenv.isLinux [...]`.
2. Darwin-specific deps: `dotnet-sdk_10` still needed. Skip all X11 / mesa / gtk / libdrm / alsa-lib / wayland — they don't exist on darwin. Graphics on mac goes via Apple frameworks, which Robust/RobustToolbox picks up at runtime if it's built to target darwin. Most of those libs may not be needed at all for a SERVER-ONLY darwin dev shell.
3. Decide scope: **server-only** on darwin (no client deps), or attempt client support. Server-only is much simpler and matches the realistic contributor path (client dev on mac needs a proper Mac build of the engine which is a separate problem). Pick server-only and document that mac contributors who need client rendering should use a Linux VM.
4. Re-enable darwin systems in `flake.nix`: `systems = inputs.flake-parts.lib.systems.flakeExposed;` or the explicit list `[ "x86_64-linux" "aarch64-linux" "x86_64-darwin" "aarch64-darwin" ]`.
5. Verify: `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes (evaluates all four systems; darwin devShell pulls fewer deps).

### Windows: WSL2 documentation path
6. No flake changes for Windows — it has no native nix. Windows contributors install WSL2 with Ubuntu, then clone the repo inside WSL and use the standard Linux nix path.
7. Add a "Windows (via WSL2)" subsection to `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`:
   - Install WSL2: `wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04`
   - Inside WSL: install nix (single-user install from https://nixos.org/download), enable flakes, install direnv
   - Clone repo inside WSL filesystem (NOT on Windows drive `/mnt/c/...` — perf disaster)
   - `direnv allow` + standard dev flow
   - Client dev from WSL: GUI apps work via WSLg (Win11+); for audio + OpenGL, this is experimental and may not work reliably. Server-only from WSL is solid.
   - Point macOS contributors who don't want a VM to a similar "server-only nix dev" experience.

### Docs + skill update
8. Update `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`:
   - Quick Start (Nix) section: mention all three platforms (Linux native, macOS native server-only, Windows via WSL2)
   - Add Windows (via WSL2) subsection
   - Note macOS server-only scope
9. Update `.claude/skills/nix/SKILL.md`:
   - Replace the "Flake is Linux-only" warning with an up-to-date matrix:
     | Platform | Native nix | Client shell | Server shell |
     | Linux    | ✅         | ✅           | ✅           |
     | macOS    | ✅         | ❌ (use VM)  | ✅           |
     | Windows  | ❌ use WSL | ❌           | ✅           |
   - Update the "When to use which path" table accordingly
10. Update `CLAUDE.md` Dev Environment section to remove the Linux-only claim and point at `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` for platform specifics.

## Out of scope
- Do not attempt to get the full client stack (graphics, audio) working on darwin or WSLg — deep rabbit hole. Document the limitation and move on.
- Do not modify anything outside `shell.nix`, `flake.nix`, docs, and the /nix skill.
- Do not bump `shell.nix` dependency versions beyond what's needed to get darwin to evaluate.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `shell.nix` branches cleanly on `stdenv.isDarwin` / `isLinux`; existing Linux entries unchanged
- [ ] `flake.nix` systems list includes all four (`x86_64-linux`, `aarch64-linux`, `x86_64-darwin`, `aarch64-darwin`)
- [ ] `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes on a Linux host (you may lack a darwin builder — evaluation alone should succeed because flake-parts doesn't require a remote builder for `check`)
- [ ] `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` has a Windows (WSL2) subsection and updated platform framing
- [ ] `.claude/skills/nix/SKILL.md` has an updated platform support matrix
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` reflects cross-platform support
- [ ] Single commit, `Bead: vs-35q` trailer, gitmoji `:sparkles:` or `:construction_worker:`

## Notes
- Windows-via-WSL2 is not really "Windows support" — it's "Linux nix running inside a Windows environment." That's fine and honest; just document clearly.
- Darwin users running this on Apple Silicon should get `aarch64-darwin` automatically.
- If `nix flake check` hits a darwin-specific missing dep we can't resolve, it's OK to conditionally exclude darwin from one specific output (e.g. drop `dev-services` on darwin if services-flake has Linux-only assumptions). Document any such exclusions in the matrix.

title: ci: build-docfx.yml fails at engine submodule update
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/build-docfx.yml` runs on schedule (`0 0 * * 0`, weekly
Sunday midnight). Latest run: **failure at step "Update Engine Submodules"**.
Subsequent steps (dotnet, build, publish docfx) all skipped.

## Probable root cause
The workflow does `git submodule update --init --recursive` then runs
custom engine-update shell. The fork only just got the RobustToolbox
gitlink committed (commit `d80851a3b` during vs-25z). The failure
pre-dates that fix and was running against a repo where the gitlink
didn't exist.

Also: publish step pushes generated docs to GitHub Pages via a PAT
(`secrets.GITHUB_PAT_DOCFX` or similar) — we likely don't have that
secret configured, so even if submodule update succeeded, publish would
fail at the PAT check.

## Investigation needed
1. Confirm whether the next scheduled run (2026-04-19 00:00 UTC)
   succeeds now that the gitlink is committed — if yes, close as fixed.
2. If still failing, inspect the step output — might be one of:
   - Missing engine-update remote / branch
   - Missing GitHub Pages publish secret
   - The workflow hardcodes upstream-specific paths
3. Decide whether we even want auto-published docfx on our fork. We
   don't have a docs site today; this was upstream's pipeline.

## Options
### Option A: disable (like labeler-review)
If we don't serve docfx-generated docs, this is pure upstream noise.
Disable via `gh api .../disable`.

### Option B: fix the secret + submodule path for our fork
Set up GH Pages on `azigler.github.io/vacation-station-14`, create the
PAT, uncomment or add a proper repository guard. Unused today so
mostly busywork.

### Option C: guard with `if: github.repository ==`
Skip on fork, preserve for upstream-sync.

## Recommendation
**A** (disable) for now. If we ever want a docfx site later, we can
revisit — it's a separate decision from "should this workflow run."

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision made + recorded
- [ ] If disable: `gh api -X PUT .../disable`, note the workflow id
- [ ] If fix: next scheduled run succeeds, docs live somewhere
- [ ] If guard: fork-case skips cleanly, no failures
notes: Post-Flavor-A: replaced with SS14 upstream's build-docfx.yml. Decision (disable / guard / fix) unchanged — we don't run a docs site. Still needs auditing.

title: ci: update-credits.yml skips on fork by design
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/update-credits.yml` runs on schedule (weekly Sunday
midnight). Latest run: **skipped**. This is NOT a failure — the workflow
has `if: github.repository == 'DeltaV-Station/Delta-v'`, so every run on
our fork correctly skips the job.

Upstream even left a comment in the file:
```
# Hey there fork dev! If you like to include your own contributors in
# this then you can probably just change this to your own repo
# Do this in dump_github_contributors.ps1 too into your own repo
```

## Options
### Option A: leave it (no action)
Harmless skips every Sunday. Doesn't pollute the failures list. Does
generate a run entry in GitHub Actions history but that's minor.

### Option B: rewrite for VS14 contributors
Flip the guard to our repo, adapt `dump_github_contributors.ps1` to
point at azigler/vacation-station-14. On success, appends new
contributors to our credits/patreons file weekly. Low-value until we
have meaningful contributor traffic.

### Option C: delete
If we're not going to run it, removing reduces workflow file count and
prevents confusion. On upstream-sync, we'd re-delete.

## Recommendation
**A** (leave). Zero cost, zero harm, preserved for future activation.
When we have a few outside contributors and want an auto-updating
credits file, flip to B with a one-line change to the guard + a script
tweak.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision recorded
- [ ] If leave: close the bead with "no action, leaving as documented
      skip"
- [ ] If rewrite: guard flipped, ps1 adapted, first run produces a PR
      or commit with current contributors
- [ ] If delete: file removed + added to upstream-sync drop list
notes: Post-Flavor-A: replaced with SS14 upstream's update-credits.yml which guards on 'space-wizards/space-station-14' (not 'DeltaV-Station/Delta-v'). Same 'skips on fork' behavior.

title: human: write rules, MOTD, server positioning, website copy, discord onboarding
description:
## Context
Hub launch, Discord bootstrap, and the website all depend on written
content — the server's identity in words. None of this is code; all of
it is YOUR voice. Can't automate what VS14 stands for.

Currently the committed config.toml.example has placeholder MOTD and no
rules. Discord has no welcome text. Website has no copy. Game MOTD is
blank or upstream default.

Each piece of copy has downstream consumers. Writing in one place and
distributing to all three (game / Discord / website) is the goal (single
source of truth — see vs-2l2 Phase 5 and vs-2dr integrations).

## Deliverables

### 1. Positioning statement (one paragraph)
The 30-second pitch. Goes on:
- Website hero
- Discord `#welcome`
- Hub tags + server description
- `ss14://` server browser blurb

Answer these in order:
- What genre of SS14 server is VS14? (mid-RP? low-RP with ambition?
  PvP-oriented? story-first?)
- What audience? (Veterans burnt out on upstream? Newbies? Delta-V
  refugees?)
- What ONE thing differentiates VS14 from the 50+ other SS14 servers?
- What will you NOT do? (No ERP. No hardcore-RP requirements. Whatever
  the negative-space is.)

Keep under 60 words for the short version; expand to 3 paragraphs for
the about page.

### 2. Server rules (markdown, `docs/community/rules.md`)
Structure (common SS14 pattern, adapt to VS14 voice):
1. **Meta rules** — no ban evasion, no harassment, follow admin
   instructions, how to appeal
2. **Roleplay expectations** — character vs player, IC/OOC separation,
   expected engagement level
3. **Conduct** — no slurs, no bigotry, how to handle conflict
4. **Antag behavior** — acceptable antag play, what crosses the line
5. **Admin escalation** — how to get an admin, what they can do, what
   they won't do
6. **Grief / self-antag** — definitions, consequences
7. **Out-of-server rules** — Discord rules apply equally; community
   extends across both
8. **Metagaming / exploiting** — bugs, client tweaks, third-party tools
9. **Account / verification** — Wizden account expectations

Look at upstream SS14 rules + Delta-V rules + three other good community
servers. Adapt, don't copy. Ban list cites rule numbers, so numbering
matters.

### 3. MOTD (short, `docs/community/motd.md`)
What players see the moment they connect. ~2-4 lines. Usually:
- Server name + fork lineage
- Link to rules
- Link to Discord
- Optional flavor line

Example shape:
```
Welcome to Vacation Station 14 — a hard fork of Delta-V.
Rules: vacation.yourdomain.com/rules | Discord: discord.gg/xxx
Have fun, respect shift structure, break character only in OOC. o7
```

### 4. Website home hero + about page (`web/content/`)
- Hero: 1-sentence pitch + CTA button ("Connect" / "Download launcher")
- About: 3-4 paragraphs — what/who/why, fork lineage, license, how to
  contribute
- Rules page: rendered from `docs/community/rules.md`
- Connect page: launcher download instructions + troubleshooting

### 5. Discord onboarding
- Server description (the short positioning statement)
- `#welcome` pinned message with rules summary + connect instructions
- Rules channel content (rendered from same source as website /rules)
- Role descriptions (what @Admin does, what @Verified is for, etc.)
- Welcome DM to new joiners

### 6. Launch announcement copy
For when you flip the hub advertise switch (vs-17n):
- Tweet / Mastodon / bluesky post
- Discord announcement (both in VS14's own server AND — if you're
  comfortable — in upstream SS14 community spaces)
- Reddit /r/ss14 post (if appropriate)
- Email / DM to Delta-V and other sibling communities for cross-promotion

## Single-source-of-truth layout
```
docs/community/
├── positioning.md          short + long versions
├── rules.md                numbered rules, canonical
├── motd.md                 compressed in-game welcome
├── about.md                 website about-page source
├── launch-announcement.md  copy drafts for launch day
└── discord/
    ├── welcome.md
    ├── roles.md
    └── rules-channel.md    (often the same as rules.md with Discord
                             formatting tweaks)
```

Consumers:
- Game server: deploys `rules.md` + `motd.md` into config.toml cvars
- Discord bot: reads from `docs/community/` at runtime
- Website: imports from `docs/community/` at build time

Edit once, ship three places.

## Writing guidance (not rules — just what tends to work)
- Avoid upstream's passive "X is not allowed"; prefer VS14's voice
  (first-person community voice — "we..." / "here we...")
- Rules should be enforceable; vague rules create drama
- Positioning should close doors ("we are NOT X") as much as open them
- Shorter MOTD is better than longer; players skim
- Humor carefully — what reads as charming to you may read as
  off-putting to a first-timer

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/community/positioning.md` — short + long versions written
- [ ] `docs/community/rules.md` — numbered, ≥ 8 sections, enforceable
- [ ] `docs/community/motd.md` — 2-4 lines
- [ ] `docs/community/about.md` — website about page
- [ ] `docs/community/discord/welcome.md` + `roles.md` + `rules-channel.md`
- [ ] `docs/community/launch-announcement.md` — copy for social posts
- [ ] All six consumed by at least one downstream service (once those
      services exist)
- [ ] First proofread pass — fresh eyes read it, flag anything weird

## Out of scope
- Visual brand (logo, color palette, fonts) — separate bead, pairs
  with vs-2dr website Phase 1 styling
- Translated copies (localization) — deferred until we have non-EN
  players asking
- Legal boilerplate (TOS, privacy) — separate bead if we start
  collecting accounts / donations

## Dependencies
None — this is writing. Blocks vs-17n, vs-2l2 Phase 3, vs-2dr Phase 2.

title: ops: stand up Robust.Cdn + switch watchdog UpdateType to Manifest (deferred)
description:
## Context
Parent: **vs-19h** (decision matrix + submodule convention) — DEFERRED.

When triggered, deploy space-wizards/Robust.Cdn via submodule at
`external/cdn/`. VS14-specific config in `ops/cdn/`. Pairs with
watchdog's Manifest update provider (we currently use Local).

## Why deferred
Local update provider works fine for single-server. Manifest requires
Robust.Cdn + CI + TLS + watchdog reconfiguration — real work but not
blocking day-one.

## Triggers to unpark
- Multiple VS14 servers (staging + prod, or cross-region)
- Frequent content updates making zip-drops painful
- Player bandwidth / latency complaints downloading client
- "Join via launcher without operator intervention" workflow

## Task (when triggered)
1. Submodule:
   ```
   git submodule add https://github.com/space-wizards/Robust.Cdn external/cdn
   ```
2. `ops/cdn/`:
   - `docker-compose.yml`
   - `appsettings.yaml` — content publish path, signing keys
     (env-loaded), manifest path
   - `Caddyfile.block` — `cdn.vacation.<domain>` with HTTPS
3. CI pipeline: on main push, package + upload to CDN's publish
   endpoint, bump manifest version
4. Watchdog config update:
   ```yaml
   UpdateType: Manifest
   Updates:
     ManifestUrl: https://cdn.vacation.<domain>/manifest.json
   ```
5. Remove the `binaries/` drop-in hack
6. `docs/upstream-sync.md` updated

## Acceptance (when triggered)
- [ ] `external/cdn/` submodule
- [ ] CDN running, Caddy-fronted, TLS
- [ ] CI publishes client on main push
- [ ] Watchdog on Manifest provider
- [ ] Clients download from CDN on connect

## Dependencies
- vs-2y8 (Caddy)
- CI that actually builds the client on push
notes:
## Service live (2026-04-13)

CDN deployed as a docker-compose service alongside observability.
Reachable at https://ss14.zig.computer/cdn/fork/vacation-station.
No builds uploaded yet; manifest endpoint returns 404 until the
first publish.

### Remaining work (to fully flip production to Manifest mode)

1. **Publish a build to the CDN**
   - Zip up our current Robust.Server_linux-x64 + SS14.Client builds
     (we already have these at /opt/vacation-station/release/)
   - Call POST /cdn/fork/vacation-station/publish with version ID +
     auth header (Authorization: Bearer $CDN_UPDATE_TOKEN)
   - Upload blob files per the publish protocol (see ForkPublishController.cs)
   - Mark publish complete

2. **Flip watchdog UpdateType: Local → Manifest**
   In /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/appsettings.yml
   (under Instances.vacation-station):
   - Change UpdateType: Local → Manifest
   - Add ManifestUrl: https://ss14.zig.computer/cdn/fork/vacation-station/manifest

3. **Restart watchdog; smoke-test launcher**
   - Game server respawns using the manifest provider
   - /info.download_url points at /cdn/fork/.../file/... URLs
   - Launcher fetches delta updates instead of the full zip

### Automation — next publish

Add a post-build hook to Content.Packaging: after it produces
release/SS14.Server_*.zip + SS14.Client.zip, automatically publish
to our CDN. This is a separate bead (vs-3mv.1 when filed).

title: ops: guidebook — render reagent/microwave/tech/lawset embeds as tables (not pills)
description:
## Context vs-1e5 shipped the guidebook; vs-mlg added entity sprite rendering. What's left: pages like Chemicals, FoodRecipes, PizzaRecipes, etc. still show empty pills for group embeds. The in-game renderer expands these to tables (reagent list, recipe list, tech tree, lawset text). Web-side just has `Name`. Audit of embed tags in ``Resources/ServerInfo/Guidebook/**/*.xml`` (still falling back to pills post-vs-mlg): | Tag | Count | Source prototype | |-------------------------------|-------|-----------------------------------------------------------| | ``GuideReagentEmbed`` | 42 | ``type: reagent`` in Resources/Prototypes/Reagents/ | | ``GuideReagentGroupEmbed`` | 22 | reagents grouped by their ``group:`` field | | ``GuideMicrowaveGroupEmbed`` | 30 | ``type: microwaveMealRecipe`` grouped by ``group:`` field | | ``GuideTechDisciplineEmbed`` | 4 | ``type: technology`` + ``type: techDiscipline`` | | ``GuideLawsetListEmbed`` | 1 | ``type: siliconLawset`` | ## Task Extend `ops/guidebook/render.py` to expand each of these embeds into a table (or similar structured layout) instead of a pill. Match the information density of the in-game version — we don't need pixel-perfect styling, but we do need the data. ### Per-tag design sketches - **GuideReagentEmbed**: single row — sprite (if any), name, description, metabolism / color / physicalDesc summary if they're short. - **GuideReagentGroupEmbed**: table of all reagents whose ``group:`` matches. Columns: icon, name, short description. Filter by the ``Group="..."`` attribute. - **GuideMicrowaveGroupEmbed**: table of all microwave recipes matching ``group:``. Columns: result entity (with sprite via vs-mlg resolver), inputs (list of reagent + solid counts), time. - **GuideTechDisciplineEmbed**: list of technologies in that discipline. Nested or flat is fine. Show name + cost + tier. - **GuideLawsetListEmbed**: show the lawset's name + the ordered list of law strings. ### Implementation notes - Reuse the existing entity-prototype scan from vs-mlg; just add reagent / recipe / technology scans alongside it. Single-pass YAML walk. - Reagent sprites come from the ``iconPath`` / ``metamorphicSprite`` fields, or fall back to the entity this reagent is stored in. Don't over-engineer — "sprite if easy, skip otherwise" is fine for v1. - Locale: reagent + recipe names live in ``Resources/Locale/en-US/**/*.ftl`` keyed by ``reagent-name-`` and similar. Resolve through the same FTL loader already used for guidebook entry titles. - Keep degraded rendering: if a group is empty (no reagents match), fall back to the pill so we don't silently ship empty tables. - CSS: add simple ``table.embed-table { ... }`` rules. Match the dark aesthetic already in style.css. ## Acceptance - [ ] Visit ``/guidebook/Chemicals.html`` — each ``

`` section (Elements, Medicine, Narcotics, …) is followed by a table of reagents with at least name + description - [ ] Visit ``/guidebook/PizzaRecipes.html`` (and other recipe pages) — recipes shown as a table with result sprite + inputs - [ ] Visit ``/guidebook/Technologies.html`` — 4 discipline sections each show a list of their technologies - [ ] ``GuideLawsetListEmbed`` on the one page that uses it renders the law text - [ ] Build time stays under 2 minutes - [ ] shellcheck + ruff clean - [ ] Fallback pill still works for unresolvable embeds ## Out of scope (file follow-ups if needed) - Full reagent-reaction chemistry graphs (the in-game version shows reaction formulas — defer unless trivial) - Gas embeds (``GuideGasEmbed`` — none used currently) - Multi-layer reagent sprite compositing ## Parent vs-z7v (community launch readiness)

title: fix: grafana postgres datasource type mismatch across prod + dev
description:
## Context
vs-2p3 + vs-13x shipped conflicting datasource types for Postgres in Grafana:

- `ops/observability/docker-compose.yml:107` env `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: grafana-postgresql-datasource` — installs the plugin
- `ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml:29` — provisions datasource with `type: postgres` (built-in)
- `ops/observability/grafana/dashboards/game-servers.json:41` (and panels in perf-metrics.json) — references `"type": "grafana-postgresql-datasource"` (plugin)

When Grafana provisions a datasource with `type: postgres` but a panel asks for `type: grafana-postgresql-datasource`, the panel fails to locate a matching datasource. In the dev services-flake stack the plugin isn't installed at all — no fallback there either.

This will silently break the Connections panel on Game Servers and any postgres-querying panel on Perf Metrics when the operator runs vs-205 verification.

## Task
Pick ONE datasource type and make all three surfaces agree. Recommend `grafana-postgresql-datasource` (the plugin) for parity with upstream SS14 dashboards — postgres-as-plugin offers query builder + TimescaleDB hooks the built-in lacks, and upstream dashboards were authored against it.

### Changes required

1. **`ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml`**: change Postgres block `type: postgres` → `type: grafana-postgresql-datasource`. Verify the rest of the block (jsonData, secureJsonData) is still compatible with the plugin schema (it should be — the plugin is a drop-in successor).

2. **`ops/observability/docker-compose.yml`**: confirm `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: grafana-postgresql-datasource` is correctly spelled and the plugin installs on grafana 11.3.0 startup. If the plugin requires a specific minimum grafana version, document it. No code change needed unless the image pin is too old.

3. **Dashboards** (`game-servers.json`, `perf-metrics.json`): already reference `grafana-postgresql-datasource` — no change needed. Do grep-verify that NO panel still references `type: postgres` by mistake; if any do, fix.

4. **Dev services-flake stack** (`flake.nix` `perSystem.process-compose."dev-services".services.grafana.graf1`): the plugin isn't in nixpkgs `grafanaPlugins`. Two options:
   - **(a) Install at runtime**: add `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS=grafana-postgresql-datasource` as an env var / arg to the dev grafana so it fetches the plugin from grafana.com on first start. Services-flake grafana module may expose this via `services.grafana.graf1.settings.plugin_install` or `extraEnv`/`env`. Check the module.
   - **(b) Omit postgres in dev**: drop the Postgres datasource from the dev flake entirely and have contributors use Prometheus + Loki only in dev. Dashboards' postgres panels would show "no datasource" in dev but that's acceptable if documented.

   Prefer **(a)** if the services-flake grafana module supports runtime plugin install; otherwise fall back to **(b)** and document clearly in `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` Dev Services Stack section.

5. **Cross-ref comment sync**: `ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml` and the dev flake's inlined grafana datasources should each carry a comment pointing at the other as the "also update me" sibling — reduces drift risk (finding #2 from vs-1bb).

### Validation
- `docker compose -f ops/observability/docker-compose.yml config` parses
- `python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml'))"` parses
- Each dashboard JSON still parses
- If option (a) chosen: `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes
- Grep the repo for any remaining `type: postgres` references that should have been updated (and any `grafana-postgresql-datasource` that shouldn't have been touched — the dashboards are correct as-is)

### Commit
One commit, gitmoji `:bug:`, trailer `Bead: vs-3oe`.

## Out of scope
- Do not change image pins unless strictly required by plugin compatibility
- Do not rewrite the dashboard JSONs structurally (they're canonical as vs-13x landed them)
- Do not migrate off `grafana-postgresql-datasource` to another postgres backend

## References
- Plugin page: https://grafana.com/grafana/plugins/grafana-postgresql-datasource/
- services-flake grafana module: https://community.flake.parts/services-flake/services/grafana

title: legal: license audit for all bundled / deployed services + LEGAL.md update
description:
## Context
We're about to deploy / bundle code from multiple upstream repositories
alongside our own fork. Each has its own license, and our obligations
differ per service (AGPL = network-service copyleft; MIT = attribution
only). Need a careful audit before public launch, not after.

**Additional context from user direction**: mirror HardLight's
README-level transparency and attribution pattern
(https://github.com/HardLightSector/HardLight/blob/master/README.md).
They do this extremely well; we should copy the pattern beat-for-beat.

## HardLight's transparency pattern — our target

### Pattern 1: Commit-hash license boundaries
HardLight documents in their README:

> "Content contributed to this repository after commit
> 2fca06eaba205ae6fe3aceb8ae2a0594f0effee0 is licensed under the GNU
> Affero General Public License version 3.0"
>
> "Content contributed to this repository before commit
> 2fca06eaba205ae6fe3aceb8ae2a0594f0effee0 is licensed under the MIT
> license"

Clean, unambiguous, machine-traceable. Anyone wanting to know what
license applies to a given file or line can run `git log --follow
<path>` and compare commit SHAs against the boundary.

### Pattern 2: Per-upstream attribution table
HardLight's README has a table with columns:
- **Subdirectory** (e.g., `_NF`, `_DV`, `_Corvax`, `_EE`)
- **Fork Name** (Frontier Station, Delta-V, Corvax, Einstein Engines)
- **Fork Repository** (URL)
- **License** (AGPL 3.0, MIT, etc.)

One row per upstream we pull from, in one place readers can scan.

### Pattern 3: Modification markers
HardLight says:
> "When we pull content from other forks, we organize their content to
> repo-specific subfolders to better track attribution and limit merge
> conflicts."
>
> "These modifications are denoted by comments around the modified
> lines."

Which maps to: `_NF/` etc. for self-contained code; inline `// NF`
comments for upstream-file modifications. Same convention we already
use for `_VS` / `_DV` — extend to every upstream we adopt.

## Task

### 1. Verify each license from primary source
For every service we deploy or bundle:
- Check `LICENSE` / `LICENSE.txt` / `COPYING` in upstream repo root
- Check the `license` field in package.json / .csproj / project.json /
  equivalent if it exists
- Record the actual license text location in the audit table
- Flag any mismatch (readme says MIT but LICENSE is GPL, etc.)

### 2. Document per-service obligations
For each license + deployment-shape combination, write down what we
must do to comply:

**AGPLv3 (cookbook, _VS code, _DV code, Starlight-derived, _NF post-hardlight-style boundary)**
- Must distribute source of our version to anyone interacting with
  the service over a network
- Must preserve copyright + license notice
- Must mark modifications
- Derivative works must be AGPLv3
- Compliance plan: all our config files live in THIS public repo;
  link to this repo from each AGPL-deployed service's UI ("Source"
  link in footer pointing at github.com/azigler/vacation-station-14)

**MIT (SS14.Admin, SS14.MapServer/MapViewer, wizard-cogs, Robust.Cdn, RSIEdit, document-simu, SS14.Changelog, most upstream SS14 pre-AGPL code)**
- Must preserve copyright notice
- Must preserve permission notice
- Compliance plan: retain upstream `LICENSE` file in any clone/copy;
  add attribution to our website's /credits page and to the README
  attribution table

**CC-BY-SA 3.0 (most SS14 assets)**
- Must credit original author
- Must share modifications under same license
- Compliance plan: ensure `_VS` assets we derive from upstream
  retain credit; assets we author from scratch MAY be AGPL-compatible
  by licensing them CC-BY-SA 3.0 (matching upstream convention)

**CC-BY-NC-SA 3.0 (some SS14 assets)**
- NC = non-commercial only. We must NOT monetize these assets.
  Per user direction, no monetization is planned, so compliant by
  default.
- Compliance plan: document in LEGAL.md that assets under this
  license are explicitly non-commercial-use-only; if we ever change
  monetization stance, must audit + remove or replace NC assets
  first.

### 3. README.md licensing section (HardLight-style)
Add a section to our README.md following HardLight's format:

**3a. License boundary**
Pick a boundary commit. Candidates:
- The initial Flavor-A paragon commit (if we execute vs-ddu) — all
  VS14-authored content since then = AGPLv3
- Or our current initial-fork commit `062c0d7a` — our authored
  content since then = AGPLv3; inherited Delta-V / upstream content
  keeps its original license (MIT + AGPL mix per Delta-V's own
  LEGAL)

Probably second option for current state, first for post-paragon.

Wording template:
```
Content authored by VS14 contributors and committed to this
repository after commit <SHA> is licensed under the GNU Affero
General Public License version 3.0 unless otherwise annotated.

Content inherited from upstream forks retains its original license
(see attribution table below). Modifications to upstream-inherited
files are marked with inline `// VS` comments and are licensed under
AGPL-3.0.
```

**3b. Attribution table**
One row per upstream we import / inherit / cherry-pick from.
Columns: Subdirectory, Fork Name, Fork Repository, License, Our
mode (hard-fork / submodule / cherry-pick / deploy-as-is).

Example:
```
| Subdirectory | Fork | Repository | License | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `_DV` | Delta-V Station | DeltaV-Station/Delta-v | AGPL-3.0 | cherry-pick |
| `_NF` | Frontier Station | new-frontiers-14/frontier-station-14 | AGPL-3.0 + MIT | cherry-pick |
| `_VS` | Vacation Station 14 (this repo) | azigler/vacation-station-14 | AGPL-3.0 | authored |
| (engine) | RobustToolbox | space-wizards/RobustToolbox | MIT | submodule pin |
| (external/cookbook) | ss14-cookbook | arimah/ss14-cookbook | AGPL-3.0 | submodule + config |
| (external/admin) | SS14.Admin | space-wizards/SS14.Admin | MIT | submodule + config |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
```

**3c. Modification annotations**
Document the `// VS` / `// DV` / `// NF` / `// EE` inline-comment
convention. One sentence; maybe a small code block example.

### 4. Per-cherry-pick attribution discipline
Every cherry-picked commit must:
- Preserve the original author line (don't squash into "VS14 import")
- Add a `Co-Authored-By:` trailer for the original author if the
  cherry-pick needed any conflict-resolution edits
- Tag the subsystem / subdirectory it lands in (e.g. "cherry-pick:
  _NF/Bank from new-frontiers-14@abc1234")
- Reference the bead that scoped the cherry-pick
- State the upstream commit SHA so anyone can trace back

This discipline fully preserves license chains. A reader running
`git log --follow Content.Server/_NF/Bank/` will see original Frontier
authors alongside our conflict-resolution commits, each SHA traceable
to its upstream.

### 5. LEGAL.md updates
Extend existing LEGAL.md with:
- The HardLight-style license-boundary statement (cross-link to
  README)
- The full attribution table (README has a summary; LEGAL.md has
  the authoritative / extended version)
- Per-service deployment licensing obligations
- Contact for license questions (email + DMCA designated agent from
  vs-1ux)

### 6. Website /credits page
(Overlaps with vs-2dr website.) Rendered from LEGAL.md data or
`docs/community/credits.md`. Auto-generated or hand-curated —
either works if the data stays in sync.

### 7. Wizden OAuth TOS check
Confirm that Wizards Den explicitly permits fork / third-party use
of their identity provider. Usually documented in their infrastructure
docs or Discord. One paragraph in LEGAL.md citing their permission.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Every service in vs-19h's matrix has its license text location
      documented (file path + commit SHA)
- [ ] LEGAL.md extended with per-service compliance section
- [ ] AGPL-network-service compliance: "View Source" link visible from
      every AGPL-deployed service (cookbook especially)
- [ ] README.md has HardLight-style license-boundary statement
- [ ] README.md has HardLight-style per-upstream attribution table
- [ ] README.md documents the `// VS` / `// DV` / `// NF` comment
      convention
- [ ] All submodule LICENSE files intact at external/<name>/LICENSE
- [ ] Website /credits page drafted (vs-2dr depends on this bead's
      output)
- [ ] Cherry-pick commit discipline documented (author preservation
      + upstream SHA reference) in CLAUDE.md and /upstream-sync skill
- [ ] Trademark/branding sanity check complete; no conflicts
- [ ] Wizden OAuth TOS confirmed as permitting fork use
- [ ] docs/upstream-sync.md lists each upstream's license column

## Dependencies
- vs-2dr (website) — /credits page lives there
- vs-7ns (brand) — brand guide confirms we're not reusing protected
  marks
- vs-1ux (user-facing legal) — feeds this; TOS / privacy policy
  written against the actual license obligations
- vs-ddu (Flavor A) — if executed, the paragon boundary commit becomes
  the natural license-transition point

## Out of scope
- Hiring a lawyer for a full licensing opinion — start with boring
  due-diligence, engage counsel only if we hit ambiguity
- Re-licensing our own code (we're AGPL; keep it)
- Re-licensing the `_DV` / `_Starlight` / upstream-SS14 code we
  inherit (not ours to relicense)

## Notes
- AGPL "triggers" on public network interaction. Private / LAN
  deployment has no AGPL obligations. Hub-advertised public server =
  AGPL obligations apply.
- MIT upstream combined with our AGPL changes = the combined work is
  AGPL (more restrictive license wins). This is why our repo is
  effectively AGPL-distributed even though imported MIT code retains
  MIT licensing internally.
- Always preserve LICENSE files in copies. Deleting them is a
  violation regardless of our own license.
- HardLight's README is the model to study:
  https://github.com/HardLightSector/HardLight/blob/master/README.md
acceptance_criteria:
- Every service's license verified from primary source (LICENSE file in upstream repo)
- LEGAL.md extended with per-service compliance section
- AGPL-network-service compliance: "View Source" link visible from cookbook deployment
- All submodule LICENSE files intact at external/<name>/LICENSE — not edited, not removed
- Website /credits page drafted (vs-2dr depends on this bead's output)
- Trademark/branding sanity check complete
- Wizden OAuth TOS confirmed as permitting fork use
- docs/upstream-sync.md lists each upstream's license column
notes:
## Progress (2026-04-12)

### Closed acceptance items
- [x] Every current upstream's license verified from primary source

- [x] RT's tri-license (MIT code + GPLv3 components + CC-BY-SA 3.0

      docs/upstream-sync.md + /upstream-sync SKILL.md both cover this

      Considered out-of-scope for this pass.
